answer_raw:
PART 1 — DECOMPOSE

11 revenue units from Note 18 and MD&A (FY2025 scale shown where available):
(A) Azure cloud services: Core AI infrastructure consumption platform; within $98,435M 'Server products and cloud services' [Note 18]; grew 34% CC FY2025 [MD&A], 39% CC Q2 FY2026 [Amy Hood].
(B) On-premises server products: Within $98,435M; declined 3% FY2025 [MD&A]; temporarily +21% CC Q2 FY2026 (SQL Server 2025, memory-price buying) [Amy Hood]; guided low-single-digit decline Q3 FY2026.
(C) Enterprise and partner services: $7,760M (+2% FY2025) [Note 18]; professional support services.
(D) M365 Commercial cloud: Within $87,767M M365 Commercial total [Note 18]; grew 15% CC FY2025 [MD&A]; 17% CC Q2 FY2026 [Amy Hood]; 450M+ seats; 15M paid Copilot seats [Q2 FY2026 call].
(E) M365 Commercial products (on-premises): Within $87,767M; grew 7% FY2025 (Office 2024 transactional boost) [MD&A]; structural shift to cloud subscription ongoing.
(F) M365 Consumer cloud: Within $7,404M Dynamics and Consumer total [Note 18]; grew 11% FY2025 [MD&A]; +29% CC Q2 FY2026 [Amy Hood]; 89M subscribers at June 30, 2025.
(G) LinkedIn: $17,812M (+9% FY2025) [Note 18]; +11% CC Q2 FY2026 [Amy Hood].
(H) Dynamics 365: Within $7,827M Dynamics total [Note 18]; grew 19% FY2025 [MD&A]; 19% CC Q2 FY2026 [Amy Hood].
(I) Windows OEM: Within $17,314M Windows and Devices [Note 18]; +3% FY2025 [MD&A]; +5% Q2 FY2026; guided ~-10% Q3 FY2026 [Amy Hood].
(J) Gaming: $23,455M (+9% FY2025) [Note 18]; Xbox content +16% FY2025; Xbox hardware -25% FY2025 [MD&A]; -9% CC Q2 FY2026 [Amy Hood].
(K) Search and news advertising: $13,878M (+20% ex-TAC FY2025) [Note 18; MD&A]; +10% CC Q2 FY2026 ex-TAC [Amy Hood].

PART 2 — APPLY ECONOMIC FRAMEWORKS PER UNIT

(A) AZURE: Demand — Structurally expanding. Satya described Microsoft Cloud surpassing $50B in a single quarter and identified 'beginning phases of AI diffusion' with TAM expanding 'across every layer of the tech stack' [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Over 250 Foundry customers on track to process 1 trillion tokens in 2026 [Satya, Q2 FY2026]; non-OpenAI commercial RPO growing 28% with broad customer/industry diversification [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026]. Complementarity — Every AI agent spawns additional CPU, container, and storage consumption beyond GPU inference [Satya Q2 FY2026 Q&A: 'AI workloads...consume large amounts of compute']; Foundry is 'a powerful on-ramp for the entire cloud — the vast majority of Foundry customers use additional Azure solutions' [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. OpenAI API exclusive on Azure ['The OpenAI API is exclusive to Azure, runs on Azure' per MD&A] locks OpenAI's compute demand into Azure. Substitution — AWS and Google Cloud are direct competitors; open-source AI models may reduce Azure premium [Risk Factors]. Supply constraints — Currently the binding constraint: 'customer demand continues to exceed our supply' [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026]; $64.6B FY2025 CapEx [Cash Flow Statement]; $37.5B Q2 FY2026 alone [Amy Hood]; $92.7B uncommenced leases [Note 13]; $32.1B construction commitments [Note 6]; $109.95B purchase commitments [MD&A]. Assumption [forward]: If GPU contracts are already 'sold for the entirety of their useful life' as management states [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 Q&A], then capacity constraints represent a timing risk rather than a structural risk — capacity additions translate directly to contracted revenue. Maya 200 (>30% TCO improvement) and Cobalt 200 (>50% CPU performance improvement) [Satya, Q2 FY2026] improve cost-per-token metrics over time, supporting margin recovery as infrastructure ages. Verdict: Net strongly positive for long-term Azure revenue growth and eventual margin improvement; primary risk is capital intensity relative to achieved monetization rate.

(B) ON-PREMISES SERVER: Demand — Structurally declining; management cited 'continued customer shift to cloud offerings' [MD&A]. Temporary positive cycles (SQL Server 2025 launch, memory-price buying) do not change the structural trajectory. Substitution — Being displaced by Azure; AI accelerates cloud migration as customers need cloud infrastructure for AI workloads. Verdict: Net negative; declining unit over 5+ year horizon.

(C) ENTERPRISE AND PARTNER SERVICES: Stable, low-growth professional services; not a primary AI growth vector. Assumption [forward]: AI implementations may require more support services, providing modest incremental demand. Verdict: Neutral to modestly positive.

(D) M365 COMMERCIAL CLOUD: Demand — Copilot drives both new seat demand and ARPU expansion on the 450M+ existing seat base. At ~3.3% Copilot penetration (derived: 15M seats / 450M+ seats per Q2 FY2026 call), ARPU expansion runway is substantial. Enterprise deployments scaling (customers with >35,000 Copilot seats tripled YoY; Publicis deploying ~95,000 seats) [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Complementarity — M365 provides the unique enterprise data context layer for agents: WorkIQ grounds agents in Teams conversations, Outlook emails, SharePoint documents, and organizational memory that no other platform can replicate at comparable scale [Satya, Q2 FY2026: 'the most important database underneath for any company']. GitHub Copilot SDK enables developers to embed WorkIQ as an MCP server, extending the data flywheel to coding workflows [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. This creates a compounding flywheel: Copilot usage → richer enterprise context → more valuable agents → more Copilot seat demand → more M365 data accumulated. Substitution — AI-native competitors to Office cited in Risk Factors ('AI-first application companies'); Google Workspace AI features. However, switching costs across 450M seats and the M365 data moat (WorkIQ) reinforce lock-in; enterprises that ground agents in M365 data create organizational dependencies that are difficult to unwind. Supply — Not supply-constrained for seat delivery; GPU allocation affects Copilot response quality but not seat issuance. Amy Hood confirmed GPUs are being allocated among M365 Copilot, Azure, and R&D [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 Q&A]. Verdict: Net strongly positive; ARPU expansion from Copilot is the primary long-term growth lever within PBP segment.

(E) M365 COMMERCIAL PRODUCTS (ON-PREMISES): Structural decline as shift to subscription cloud continues. Office 2024 transactional purchasing created a temporary +7% FY2025 uplift [MD&A]. AI will not rescue on-premises licensing. Verdict: Declining unit over 5+ years; shrinking as a share of M365 revenue.

(F) M365 CONSUMER CLOUD: Demand — Moderate; 89M subscribers; ARPU-driven growth from January 2025 price increase; Copilot app daily users up ~3x YoY [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Complementarity — Copilot Checkout integration with PayPal, Shopify, and Stripe creates new consumer agentic commerce monetization [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Substitution — Free Google Workspace consumer products, Apple productivity apps, free AI tools pose competitive risk. Verdict: Moderate positive; ARPU-driven with some substitution headwind; lower strategic priority than commercial.

(G) LINKEDIN: Demand — Consistent; 9% FY2025, 11% CC Q2 FY2026; Marketing Solutions with 30% growth in paid video ads; double-digit member growth [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Complementarity — AI-enhanced talent matching, marketing targeting, and Sales Solutions insights add value. Substitution — AI-native recruiting and HR tools represent a medium-term competitive risk [Risk Factors]. Risk — GDPR fine from Irish Data Protection Commission (appeal pending, December 2025 hearing [Note 14]) could constrain targeted advertising model and create regulatory precedent. Verdict: Moderate positive; mid-single to low-double-digit growth sustainable; GDPR regulatory risk is a material downside factor.

(H) DYNAMICS 365: Demand — ERP/CRM with AI agents directly integrated; 19% growth sustained across FY2025 and Q2 FY2026 [MD&A; Amy Hood]; AI agents for sales qualification and knowledge management [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Complementarity — Agents built into the suite enhance value and switching costs. Substitution — Salesforce, SAP, Oracle all competing with AI-integrated offerings [Risk Factors]. Verdict: Net positive; high-teens growth sustainable if AI agent differentiation is maintained.

(I) WINDOWS OEM: Demand — Correlated to PC market cycle. AI PC (Copilot+) cited as a potential new hardware refresh cycle driver ['Growth of the AI PC category' per Item 1]; Windows 11 at 1B users (+45% YoY) indicates broad installed base [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Windows 10 EOS benefit is temporary (Q3 FY2026 OEM guided -10%) [Amy Hood]. Memory price increases add PC market uncertainty [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026]. Substitution — Mobile platforms and alternative OS remain structural threats [Risk Factors]. Assumption [forward]: If AI PC category drives a sustained multi-year hardware refresh cycle, Windows OEM could outperform the general PC market. Verdict: Mixed; structural cyclicality and substitution are persistent; AI PC cycle could provide medium-term positive offset.

(J) GAMING: Demand — Underperforming; Q2 FY2026 gaming -9% CC, with first-party content execution failures and impairment charges [Amy Hood; Satya, Q2 FY2026]; Xbox hardware -25% FY2025 [MD&A]. Complementarity — AI in game content creation is nascent and not yet a disclosed growth driver in the materials. Substitution — PlayStation, Nintendo, mobile gaming, streaming platforms [Risk Factors]. Assumption [forward]: If Activision mobile (King) and Xbox Cloud Gaming (record paid streaming hours noted [Satya, Q2 FY2026]) can offset console hardware declines, gaming could stabilize over a multi-year horizon. Verdict: Near-term headwind; long-term uncertain pending execution on mobile and cloud gaming strategy.

(K) SEARCH AND NEWS ADVERTISING: Demand — Slowing from +20% ex-TAC (FY2025) to +10% CC (Q2 FY2026) as third-party partnership benefits normalize; some execution challenges noted [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026]; Bing and Edge share gains cited [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Complementarity — AI-enhanced Bing with Copilot and Copilot Checkout integrations evolve search into an agentic commerce platform [Satya, Q2 FY2026]. Substitution — AI-native conversational search (ChatGPT, Google AI Overviews) poses the most acute risk — Risk Factors cite 'AI-first application companies' as search competitors; if AI-driven answers reduce traditional search query volume, the existing CPM advertising model is structurally challenged. Assumption [forward]: Microsoft's agentic commerce model (Copilot Checkout with PayPal/Shopify/Stripe) could partially offset traditional search revenue declines, but the monetization model is unproven at scale. Verdict: Mixed; AI creates both opportunity (agentic commerce) and existential substitution risk (conversational search).

PART 3 — SYNTHESIZE

Thesis: BULLISH ON MICROSOFT'S LONG-TERM TRAJECTORY, WITH MATERIAL NEAR-TERM MARGIN COMPRESSION AND CONCENTRATED RISKS IN CAPITAL INTENSITY, OPENAI DEPENDENCE, AND CONSUMER/GAMING SEGMENTS.

Core bull mechanism: Azure (unit A) and M365 Commercial cloud (unit D) are mutually reinforcing through AI diffusion via a compounding flywheel: enterprise AI diffusion → more Azure consumption for agent workloads and Foundry → Microsoft's exclusive OpenAI API strengthens the platform moat [MD&A] → more enterprise developer adoption of Foundry → more Copilot usage by knowledge workers → richer M365 WorkIQ organizational context → higher Copilot ARPU on 450M+ commercial seats → higher M365 Commercial cloud revenue growth. This mechanism is grounded in management's disclosure that agents 'need to be grounded in enterprise data and knowledge' and that M365 data is 'the most important database underneath for any company' [Satya, Q2 FY2026 Q&A], making M365 complementary to, not substituted by, Azure AI scale. The flywheel is reinforced by the scale of already-contracted revenue: commercial RPO of $625B at Q2 FY2026 (up from $368B at June 30, 2025 [Note 12]) with GPU contracts 'sold for the entirety of their useful life' [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 Q&A], providing revenue visibility across the CapEx depreciation cycle.

Gross margin trajectory: Declining near-term — Microsoft Cloud gross margin was 69% in FY2025 [MD&A] and guided ~65% in Q3 FY2026 [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026]. Assumption [forward]: If Maya 200/Cobalt 200 efficiency gains compound and AI infrastructure depreciates over six years [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026: 'depreciate over six years'] while revenue grows through Copilot ARPU expansion and Azure consumption growth, gross margins should recover in the latter half of the decade — consistent with the observed trajectory in Azure's CPU fleet where 'margins actually improve with time' [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 Q&A]. This is internally consistent: the near-term margin compression is a deliberate investment in capacity that, once contracted and monetized, should generate improving margins per unit as software optimization and asset aging reduce cost-per-token.

Bear risks and internal consistency caveats: (1) Capital intensity: $64.6B FY2025 CapEx, $37.5B in a single quarter [Q2 FY2026], and >$230B in forward commitments [Notes 6, 13; MD&A] create substantial execution risk if AI revenue growth moderates or open-source model efficiency reduces Azure pricing power [Risk Factors]. (2) OpenAI concentration: 45% of $625B commercial RPO from a single customer/partner [Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026]; a change in OpenAI's business model, financial capacity, or cloud provider relationships would materially impact both Azure revenue and the equity method investment (OpenAI losses drove -$4,725M in FY2025 Other, net [Note 3]). (3) IRS dispute: $28.9B proposed additional taxes plus penalties [Note 11] is a material unaccrued contingency that could represent a significant cash outflow if resolved adversely. (4) Search substitution: AI-first conversational search could structurally reduce the search advertising TAM [Risk Factors]; agentic commerce (Copilot Checkout) is a promising pivot but unproven at scale. (5) Gaming and on-premises server are declining or underperforming units providing revenue mix drag. (6) AI substitution in productivity tools: Risk Factors explicitly cite 'AI-first application companies' as Office competitors; if the switching cost moat proves insufficient, M365 seat growth and ARPU could disappoint.

Internally consistent conclusion: Over a 5+ year horizon, Azure and M365 Copilot are the dominant value creation engines, with Dynamics 365, LinkedIn, Fabric, and GitHub as meaningful secondary contributors. Assuming [forward assumption] AI diffusion continues at the pace evidenced by 39% Azure growth and 160% Copilot seat growth, and assuming [forward assumption] that the WorkIQ enterprise data moat and exclusive OpenAI partnership prove durable, Microsoft's revenue should compound at mid-to-high double-digit rates with eventual gross margin recovery. The central long-term uncertainty is whether the extraordinary capital intensity ($230B+ in forward commitments) is fully justified by Copilot ARPU penetration and Azure AI pricing trajectories — early evidence (15M Copilot seats at ~3.3% penetration of 450M+ seats, $625B RPO, 80% Fortune 500 using Copilot Studio [Satya, Q2 FY2026]) is directionally positive, but the full monetization curve across the useful life of FY2025-FY2026 CapEx will not be observable for several years.

answer_raw: PROFITABILITY: FY2025 results were strong and broad-based. Total revenue was $281,724M (+15% YoY), operating income was $128,528M (+17%), and net income was $101,832M (+16%), with diluted EPS of $13.64 (+16%) [Income Statements, Part II Item 8]. Operating margin was approximately 45.6% (128,528/281,724), reflecting operating leverage as income grew faster than revenue. Gross margin was $193,893M (68.8% of revenue). By segment: Productivity and Business Processes operating margin was approximately 57.8% (69,773/120,810); Intelligent Cloud approximately 42.0% (44,589/106,265); More Personal Computing approximately 25.9% (14,166/54,649) [MD&A Segment Results, Part II Item 7]. Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage declined to 69%, driven by AI infrastructure scaling, partially offset by efficiency gains in Azure and M365 Commercial cloud [MD&A Overview]. The effective tax rate was 17.6% (provision $21,795M / pre-tax income $123,627M), benefiting primarily from earnings taxed at lower rates through the Ireland regional operations center, which generated 81% of foreign income before tax in FY2025 [Note 11]. A material headwind was Other income (expense), net of -$4,901M (vs. -$1,646M in FY2024), driven by -$4,725M in Other, net reflecting net recognized losses on equity method investments including OpenAI [Note 3; MD&A Other Income section]. CASH FLOW QUALITY: Operating cash flow was $136,162M (up from $118,548M in FY2024), well above net income, aided by $34,153M in depreciation/amortization and $11,974M in stock-based compensation [Cash Flows Statements]. Capital expenditures (additions to property and equipment) were $64,551M, up 45% from $44,477M in FY2024, driven by accelerating AI infrastructure investment [Cash Flows Statements]. Implied free cash flow (CFO minus CapEx) was approximately $71.6B. Cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments grew to $94,565M from $75,543M [Balance Sheet]. The large gap between net income and CFO reflects the scale of non-cash D&A consistent with a capital-intensive business in investment mode. CAPITAL STRUCTURE AND ALLOCATION: Long-term debt face value stands at $49,206M (carrying value $43,151M; current portion $2,999M), and the company is rated AAA [Note 10; Note 5 credit-risk contingent features]. Cash and investments of $94.6B comfortably exceed total debt obligations, maintaining a net-cash-positive balance sheet. Finance lease liabilities surged to $46,172M from $27,145M a year earlier, reflecting rapid datacenter expansion through lease financing [Note 13]. Operating lease liabilities were $22,861M [Note 13]. Critically, $92.7B in additional primarily datacenter-related leases had not yet commenced as of June 30, 2025, representing a substantial forward fixed-cost commitment building into the cost structure through FY2031 [Note 13]. Combined contractual obligations total approximately $397B, including $109,953M in purchase commitments (primarily datacenter-related), $32,149M in construction commitments, and $178,701M in operating and finance lease obligations including imputed interest [MD&A Contractual Obligations table]. On capital return: dividends declared totaled $24,678M ($3.32/share for FY2025) and share repurchases totaled $13,000M (31 million shares) [Note 15; Cash Flows]. A new $60.0B share repurchase program has $57.3B remaining as of June 30, 2025 [Note 15]. Total stockholders equity is $343,479M [Balance Sheet]. MATERIAL RISKS AND HEADWINDS: (1) IRS Tax Exposure: NOPAs for FY2004-2013 seek $28.9B in additional tax plus penalties and interest on intercompany transfer pricing; Microsoft contests the adjustments and expects no near-term resolution [Note 11; MD&A Income Taxes]. Gross unrecognized tax benefits total $24.7B with $8.2B in accrued interest expense net of tax benefits [Note 11]. (2) Capital Intensity and ROIC Risk: CapEx increased from $28,107M (FY2023) to $44,477M (FY2024) to $64,551M (FY2025), a 130% increase over two years [Cash Flows Statements]. Finance lease obligations nearly doubled YoY. The MD&A explicitly warns that investments in cloud and AI infrastructure will continue to increase operating costs and may decrease operating margins [MD&A Economic Conditions]. (3) AI Infrastructure Gross Margin Pressure: Cloud GM% declined to 69% in FY2025 [MD&A Overview]; Q3 FY2026 guidance calls for approximately 65% [Q2 FY2026 Earnings Call, Hood]. (4) OpenAI Equity Method Losses: Other, net deteriorated from -$1,319M (FY2024) to -$4,725M (FY2025), introducing growing earnings volatility below operating income [Note 3]. (5) Legal Contingencies: Accrued legal liabilities of $541M with potential additional adverse outcomes approximately $600M beyond recorded amounts noted as reasonably possible [Note 14]. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: Microsoft's financial health is robust at the operating level: AAA-rated balance sheet, net-cash-positive position ($94.6B vs. $43.2B carrying value of long-term debt), $136.2B in operating cash generation, and broad-based profitability across all three segments. The primary tension is between accelerating capital commitments required to meet AI demand and near-term gross margin compression. This investment is intentional and management-guided, but ROIC on the unprecedented capital deployment has yet to be demonstrated over the 6-year hardware useful life of assets being acquired.

answer_raw: PRODUCTIVITY AND BUSINESS PROCESSES (PBP) — PRIMARY GROWTH DRIVER WITH HIGHEST MARGINS: FY2025 revenue was $120,810M (+13% YoY), operating income was $69,773M (+17%), and operating margin was approximately 57.8% [Segment Results, MD&A Part II Item 7]. Microsoft 365 Commercial products and cloud services grew 14% (+$10.8B), with commercial cloud growing 15% — driven by 6% seat growth (primarily small/medium businesses and frontline workers) and ARPU expansion from E5 and Copilot premium tiers [MD&A PBP]. M365 Consumer cloud grew 11% driven by 8% subscriber growth to 89.0 million and a January 2025 price increase [MD&A PBP]. LinkedIn grew 9% with all four lines of business growing [MD&A PBP]. Dynamics 365 grew 19% across all workloads [MD&A PBP]. Cost of revenue grew 14% and gross margin increased $11.2B (+13%); gross margin percentage decreased slightly due to AI infrastructure scaling, partially offset by M365 Commercial cloud efficiency gains [MD&A PBP]. In Q2 FY2026, PBP delivered $34.1B revenue (+16% constant currency) with operating margin at 60% — expanding year-over-year — driven by Copilot and E5 momentum [Earnings Call, Hood]. M365 Commercial cloud grew 17% CC; M365 Consumer cloud grew 29% CC; LinkedIn grew 11% CC; Dynamics 365 grew 19% CC [Earnings Call, Hood]. Copilot defines management's strategic narrative for the segment: 15 million paid M365 Copilot seats (seat additions up over 160% YoY); number of customers with 35,000+ seats tripled YoY; daily active users increased 10x YoY; average conversations per user doubled YoY [Earnings Call, Nadella]. Notable large deployments include Publicis (95,000+ seats), Fiserv, ING, University of Kentucky, and US Department of Interior [Earnings Call, Nadella]. GitHub Copilot reached 4.7 million paid subscribers (+75% YoY), with Copilot Pro Plus individual subscriptions growing 77% sequentially [Earnings Call, Nadella]. WorkIQ — described as grounding agents in M365 organizational data (emails, meetings, documents, relationships, project history) — was introduced as the most valuable stateful agent for organizations and is described as delivering faster and more accurate work-grounded results than competition [Earnings Call, Nadella]. Q3 FY2026 M365 Commercial cloud guidance: 13-14% CC growth with continued Copilot and E5 momentum [Earnings Call, Hood]. INTELLIGENT CLOUD — FASTEST-GROWING, MOST CAPITAL-INTENSIVE, SUPPLY-CONSTRAINED: FY2025 revenue was $106,265M (+21% YoY), operating income was $44,589M (+18%), and operating margin was approximately 42.0% [Segment Results, MD&A Part II Item 7]. Azure and other cloud services grew 34% (driven by demand across the portfolio of AI and cloud services); server products declined 3% as customers continued shifting to cloud; enterprise and partner services grew 2% [MD&A Intelligent Cloud]. The most significant headwind: cost of revenue grew 36% against 21% revenue growth — the largest cost-revenue imbalance of any segment — driven by AI infrastructure investment, compressing segment gross margin percentage materially [MD&A Intelligent Cloud]. In Q2 FY2026, Intelligent Cloud delivered $32.9B revenue (+29% CC) with Azure growing 39% CC slightly ahead of expectations [Earnings Call, Hood]. On-premises server revenue grew 21% CC ahead of expectations from SQL Server 2025 launch and pre-buying ahead of memory price increases; this is expected to normalize to low-single-digit decline in Q3 FY2026 [Earnings Call, Hood]. Segment operating margin in Q2 FY2026 was 42%, down slightly YoY as AI investments were mostly offset by improved operating leverage [Earnings Call, Hood]. The defining strategic constraint is supply: Hood explicitly stated demand continues to exceed available supply, and that if all GPU additions in Q1/Q2 FY2026 had been allocated to Azure, revenue growth would have exceeded 40% rather than the reported 39% [Earnings Call, Hood Q&A response to Keith Weiss]. GPU capacity is deliberately allocated in priority order across first-party Copilot services (M365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot, Dragon Copilot), R&D capacity, and then Azure third-party demand — an intentional portfolio optimization for long-term LTV rather than short-term Azure revenue maximization [Earnings Call, Hood and Nadella Q&A]. Commercial RPO reached $625B as of Q2 FY2026 (up 11% YoY; approximately 45% from OpenAI; remaining approximately 55% growing 28%); weighted average duration extended to approximately 2.5 years; roughly 25% recognized in the next 12 months, up 39% YoY [Earnings Call, Hood]. Strategic priorities: Maya 200 custom accelerator (30%+ TCO improvement vs. prior fleet hardware; deployed for inferencing and synthetic data generation for the superintelligence team, and for Copilot and Foundry inferencing) [Earnings Call, Nadella]; Cobalt 200 CPU (50%+ performance improvement vs. first custom processor) [Earnings Call, Nadella]; Fairwater AI super factory linking Atlanta and Wisconsin sites through an AI WAN with liquid cooling for higher GPU density [Earnings Call, Nadella]; sovereign cloud deployments announced in 7 countries in Q2 FY2026 [Earnings Call, Nadella]; Fabric analytics platform ($2B+ annualized run rate, 60% YoY revenue growth, 31,000+ customers) as an Azure on-ramp where the vast majority of Fabric customers use additional Azure solutions [Earnings Call, Nadella]. Q3 FY2026 Azure guidance: 37-38% CC; Intelligent Cloud segment revenue guided at $34.1-34.4B [Earnings Call, Hood]. MORE PERSONAL COMPUTING (MPC) — MIXED RESULTS, GAMING AS KEY HEADWIND: FY2025 revenue was $54,649M (+7% YoY), operating income was $14,166M (+18%), and operating margin was approximately 25.9% [Segment Results, MD&A Part II Item 7]. The 7% FY2025 growth was driven by Gaming (+9%, largely from the full-year Activision Blizzard contribution vs. partial year in FY2024 given the October 13, 2023 acquisition date [Note 7]), Search and news advertising (+13%; ex-TAC +20%), and Windows OEM and Devices (+3%) [MD&A MPC]. Xbox hardware declined 25% [MD&A MPC]. Gross margin percentage improved as mix shifted to higher-margin businesses [MD&A MPC]. In Q2 FY2026, MPC revenue declined 3% to $14.3B — a deterioration from FY2025 [Earnings Call, Hood]. Gaming declined 9% CC and Xbox content and services declined 5% CC, below expectations due to first-party content weakness with platform-wide impact; management recognized gaming business impairment charges in Q2 FY2026 [Earnings Call, Hood]. Windows OEM grew 5% in Q2 FY2026 (ahead of expectations) benefiting from Windows 10 end-of-support and pre-buying ahead of memory price increases; Q3 FY2026 guidance is Windows OEM and Devices declining low-teens as inventory normalizes and the Windows 10 benefit fades [Earnings Call, Hood].
